title: Cephaelis domingensis
taxonomy: Cephaelis domingensis is a species of flowering plant in the family Rubiaceae. The genus Cephaelis is part of the tribe Psychotrieae within the family. It is a member of the order Gentianales and the clade asterids. As a member of the Rubiaceae family, Cephaelis domingensis is related to other plants in the same family, such as gardenias and coffee. The classification of Cephaelis domingensis is based on its morphological characteristics and phylogenetic relationships with other Rubiaceae species.
uses: Cephaelis domingensis, also known as the Jamaica dogwood, has been traditionally used in folk medicine for various purposes. The plant contains a compound called scopoletin, which has been shown to have potential anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. In traditional medicine, Cephaelis domingensis is used to treat conditions such as fever, rheumatism, and even snake bites. Additionally, the plant has been used to relieve pain and reduce anxiety. However, more research is needed to fully understand its medicinal effects and potential side effects.

constituents: Cephaelis domingensis, also known as the Jamaican dogwood, contains the active constituents cymarine and nuciferine, which have been traditionally used for their medicinal properties. Cymarine has been studied for its potential anti-inflammatory and anti-anxiety effects. Nuciferine has been found to have antispasmodic and analgesic properties. intro: Cephaelis domingensis, also known as Dominican guava or yellow guava, is a small shrub native to the Dominican Republic and other Caribbean islands, belonging to the family Rubiaceae. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, offering health benefits such as anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, which can aid in the treatment of various conditions, including wounds and gastrointestinal issues. The bioactive constituents of Cephaelis domingensis include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and alkaloids, which are responsible for its therapeutic properties. In traditional medicine, Cephaelis domingensis is often prepared as a tea or infusion, as well as being used in the form of tinctures and extracts, to take advantage of its medicinal properties.
